name: Gironde Bastides and Vineyards Loop
name_original: Sortie vallonnée à la découverte des ruines
description: This difficult 64.0-mile road cycling route offers a hilly outing discovering historical ruins like the Templar Commandery of Sallebruneau.
region_intro: Road cycling around Sauveterre-De-Guyenne, located in France's Entre-deux-Mers region, features a landscape characterized by rolling hills and extensive vineyards. The area offers a network of routes that traverse agricultural lands, forests, and dedicated greenways. Cyclists can explore varied terrain, from the repurposed railway line of the Roger Lapébie track to routes that approach the Garonne river valley. Best road cycling routes around Sauveterre-De-Guyenne The most popular road cycling route is Gironde Bastides and Vineyards Loop, a 64.0 miles (103.1 km) trail that takes 4 hours 6 minutes to complete. This difficult route connects historical bastide towns and winds through the region's vineyards. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Sauve Majeure tunnel – Roger Lapébie Cycle Path loop from Sauveterre-de-Guyenne, a moderate 40.5 miles (65.1 km) path. This route utilizes the Roger Lapébie track, offering a ride through forested sections. Local road cyclists also love the Piste Roger Lapébie – Bivouac Area – Piste Lapébie loop from Sauveterre-de-Guyenne, a 22.6 miles (36.4 km) trail leading through the Roger Lapébie track, often completed in about 1 hour 26 minutes. Road cycling around Sauveterre-De-Guyenne is defined by rolling hills, extensive vineyards, and dedicated greenways like the Roger Lapébie track. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy rides to more challenging routes. The routes in Sauveterre-De-Guyenne are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 27 reviews. More than 580 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Sauveterre-De-Guyenne's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Sauveterre-De-Guyenne?
A: There are over 90 road cycling routes around Sauveterre-De-Guyenne, offering a wide variety of options for different skill levels and preferences. These routes traverse the region's characteristic rolling hills and extensive vineyards.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in the Entre-deux-Mers region?
A: The Entre-deux-Mers region, where Sauveterre-De-Guyenne is located, is known for its rolling hills and vast vineyards, often described as an 'ocean of vines.' You'll find a mix of dedicated greenways, quiet country roads, and some routes that feature more challenging terrain as they approach the Garonne river valley. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e843288442/gironde-bastides-and-vineyards-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gironde Bastides and Vineyards Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for example, includes over 1000m of ascent.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly road cycling routes in Sauveterre-De-Guyenne?
A: Yes, the area offers several easy routes suitable for families or those seeking a more relaxed ride. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41489565" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piste Roger Lapébie – Bivouac Area – Piste Lapébie loop from Sauveterre-de-Guyenne&lt;/a&gt;, which utilizes the Roger Lapébie track. This greenway, built on an old railway line, provides a very pleasant and safe cycling experience through forested sections, connecting Sauveterre-de-Guyenne to Créon.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e843288442/gironde-bastides-and-vineyards-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gironde Bastides and Vineyards Loop&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain, with over 1000m of ascent over 103 km. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/35814660" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Martin Church of Arbi – View of Saint-Macaire loop from Sauveterre-de-Guyenne&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 113 km and features similar elevation changes, taking you through varied landscapes and offering panoramic views.

Q: What historical sites or landmarks can I see along the cycling routes?
A: Sauveterre-De-Guyenne itself is a medieval bastide town with a distinctive grid layout and 14th-century arcades. You can explore the Church of Notre Dame and the well-preserved &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6803015" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Porte Saubotte&lt;/a&gt;. Further afield, routes can lead you to the ruins of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/563945" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Abbey of La Sauve-Majeure&lt;/a&gt;, known for its Romanesque architecture, or the charming town of La Réole, a 'Ville d'Art et d'Histoire' with views across the Garonne valley. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40967987" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sauve Majeure tunnel – Roger Lapébie Cycle Path loop from Sauveterre-de-Guyenne&lt;/a&gt; is a great way to combine cycling with historical exploration.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in the region are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e843288442/gironde-bastides-and-vineyards-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gironde Bastides and Vineyards Loop&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40967987" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sauve Majeure tunnel – Roger Lapébie Cycle Path loop from Sauveterre-de-Guyenne&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/29069999" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Branne Bridge – Dordogne at Castillon loop from Blasimon&lt;/a&gt;. These circular routes make planning your ride convenient.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Sauveterre-De-Guyenne?
A: The region is generally pleasant for cycling from spring through autumn. Spring offers lush green vineyards and mild temperatures, while autumn provides beautiful colors and comfortable riding conditions. Summer can be warm, but early mornings or late afternoons are ideal for enjoying the routes through the 'ocean of vines' and forested sections.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or accommodation along the routes?
A: Yes, the region is dotted with charming villages and towns where you can find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. Sauveterre-De-Guyenne itself has a central market square with arcades, perfect for a break. The weekly market in the Place de la Republique offers a taste of local life. Many routes pass through or near these communities, providing opportunities to refuel and rest.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Sauveterre-De-Guyenne?
A: The road cycling routes around Sauveterre-De-Guyenne are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 27 reviews. Cyclists often praise the picturesque landscapes, particularly the extensive vineyards and the well-maintained greenways like the Roger Lapébie track, which offers a safe and enjoyable ride through forests.

Q: Is there public transport or parking available for cyclists?
A: While specific public transport options directly tailored for cyclists to reach trailheads might be limited, Sauveterre-De-Guyenne is accessible by road, and parking is generally available within the town. Many routes start directly from the town center, making it convenient to park and begin your ride. For longer routes or connections to other towns, consider checking local bus services or train lines that might accommodate bikes, though this can vary.

Q: Can I combine road cycling with other activities in the region?
A: Absolutely! The Entre-deux-Mers region offers a rich blend of activities. Beyond cycling, you can explore the medieval bastide town of Sauveterre-De-Guyenne, visit local wineries for tastings, or discover other historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3539806" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Château de Rauzan&lt;/a&gt;. The natural beauty of the vineyards and forests also provides opportunities for photography and enjoying the tranquil French countryside.
tips:
Although very damaged, its tranquility and calm largely deserve the discovery. Founded in 1214 by the Templars, located on a feudal motte, it will be taken over by the Hospitallers in 1296.
There are only a few vestiges of the church, the castle, and the garden of medicinal plants has been reconstructed.

faqs.question: How challenging is the "Hilly outing discovering the ruins" racebike route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as difficult. With over 1,000 meters of elevation gain across its 103 km distance, it features significant climbs and hilly terrain. It is best suited for experienced cyclists looking for a demanding ride.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain will I encounter on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: Expect a hilly to mountainous terrain with substantial elevation changes, including punchy uphills and downhills. The road surfaces can vary, from well-maintained asphalt on main roads to potentially older, less-perfect pavement on quieter, scenic sections.

faqs.question: What historical sites and landmarks can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route allows you to discover fascinating historical sites. You'll pass by the Templar Commandery of Sallebruneau and the Bagas fortified mill., offering unique glimpses into the past.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to ride this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to ride this route is typically during spring or autumn. These seasons offer more moderate temperatures, which are ideal for tackling the hilly terrain. Summer can be very hot, and winter might bring challenging weather conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are mentioned for cycling this route. However, if you plan to enter any private historical sites or attractions along the way, they may have their own admission fees.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route?
faqs.answer: While a specific trailhead parking isn't detailed, you can typically find parking in the towns or villages near the start of the route. Look for public parking areas or consider starting from a location with amenities like cafes or shops where you can safely leave your vehicle.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating, significant distance (over 100 km), and substantial elevation gain (over 1,000 m), this route is not recommended for beginners. It requires a good level of fitness and cycling experience.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect while cycling?
faqs.answer: You can anticipate diverse and often breathtaking landscapes. The route offers a blend of natural beauty, potentially including forested areas and picturesque valleys, serving as a scenic backdrop to the historical ruins you'll discover. Climbing to the ruins often rewards you with panoramic vistas.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several official trails. You'll find sections that overlap with the Étape 9 • Chemin d'Amadour, Voie de Vézelay Ouest, Bergerac - Mont-de-Marsan, De Sauveterre au Canal de Garonne à vélo, and Le Tour de Gironde à Vélo - Étape 2 (Sauveterre-de-Guyenne - Bazas), among others.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for racebiking on public roads and paths. While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on the route itself, it's generally not suitable for cycling with dogs due to the distance, difficulty, and road conditions. If you plan to visit any specific historical sites, check their individual pet policies.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hilly racebike outing?
faqs.answer: For this challenging route, bring plenty of water and snacks, appropriate cycling gear including a helmet, repair kit, and spare tubes. Given the varied terrain and potential for less-perfect pavement, a well-maintained racebike with suitable gearing for climbs is essential. Don't forget navigation tools and a fully charged phone.
